coredata.dc:title: A single-component sensor array to detect multiple antibiotics via dual-emission chlorophyll carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Wenbang
Lu, Yifan
Wei, Zihan
Tang, Jing
Zhang, Min
coredata.dc:description: In this study, dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) were fabricated using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal synthesis with natural chlorophyll as the carbon precursor. These DE-CDs not only preserve the porphyrin structure of chlorophyll, exhibiting notable fluorescence at 682 nm, but they also display the characteristic fluorescence emission of conventional CDs at 450 nm. Leveraging the unique dual-emission properties of DE-CDs, we have engineered a single-component fluorescent sensor array for the high-throughput detection of antibiotics, which are emerging contaminants in water. The fluorescence responses (F450, F682, and the F450/F682 ratio) of the DE-CDs toward various antibiotics were analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA), enabling the facile differentiation of multiple antibiotics with good reproducibility achieved in real water samples. This innovative DE-CDs approach eliminates the need for multiple sensing probes and offers a practical concept for the creation of single-component sensing platforms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual-emission carbon dots
Single-component sensor array
Antibiotics
Fluorescence detection
Principal component analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent assay of antibiotics by DE-CDs sensor array
Morphology and structure characterization of DE-CDs
The discrimination of antibiotics by using DE-CDs
The detection mechanism of DE-CDs towards antibiotics
The detection of antibiotics in practical environmental samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the DE-CDs. (a) TEM images of DE-CDs. (b) HRTEM images of DE-CDs. (c) UV-absorption spectrum of DE-CDs. (d) FTIR spectrum of DE-CDs. (e) XPS spectrum of DE-CDs. (f−h) XPS spectrum of (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s and (h) O 1s peaks of DE-CDs. (i) Emission spectrum of DE-CDs with increasing excitation wavelength from 330 nm to 430 nm.

The discrimination of antibiotics by uisng DE-CDs. (a) Histogram and (b) PCA scatter plot of fluorescence response patterns of DE-CDs towards 11 antibiotics (final concentration = 100 μM). (c) Histogram and (d) PCA scatter plot of fluorescence response patterns of DE-CDs towards 11 antibiotics (final concentration = 10 μM).

Discrimination of various concentrations of antibiotics by using DE-CDs. Histogram of DE-CDs sensor array towards (a) MOX and (d) CTC. 2D PCA scatter plot of DE-CDs sensor array towards (b) MOX and (e) CTC. Plots of PC1 vs the concentrations of (c) MOX and (f) CTC.

Discrimination of antibiotics mixtures (the sum of the final concentration equals to 20 μM). (a) Histogram of fluorescence response patterns of DE-CDs towards MOX and CTC. (b) 2D PCA scatter plot of DE-CDs towards MOX and CTC.
A single-component sensor array of DE-CDs synthesized by hydrothermal method using spinach chlorophyll for antibiotic detection.
Detection of antibiotics in river samples using DE-CDs sensor array.

head.ce:title: A single-component sensor array to detect multiple antibiotics via dual-emission chlorophyll carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Shanghai Key Laboratory for Urban Ecological Processes and Eco-Restoration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) were fabricated using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal synthesis with natural chlorophyll as the carbon precursor. These DE-CDs not only preserve the porphyrin structure of chlorophyll, exhibiting notable fluorescence at 682 nm, but they also display the characteristic fluorescence emission of conventional CDs at 450 nm. Leveraging the unique dual-emission properties of DE-CDs, we have engineered a single-component fluorescent sensor array for the high-throughput detection of antibiotics, which are emerging contaminants in water. The fluorescence responses (F450, F682, and the F450/F682 ratio) of the DE-CDs toward various antibiotics were analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA), enabling the facile differentiation of multiple antibiotics with good reproducibility achieved in real water samples. This innovative DE-CDs approach eliminates the need for multiple sensing probes and offers a practical concept for the creation of single-component sensing platforms.

In this study, dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) were fabricated using a straightforward one-step hydrothermal synthesis with natural chlorophyll as the carbon precursor. These DE-CDs not only preserve the porphyrin structure of chlorophyll, exhibiting notable fluorescence at 682 nm, but they also display the characteristic fluorescence emission of conventional CDs at 450 nm. Leveraging the unique dual-emission properties of DE-CDs, we have engineered a single-component fluorescent sensor array for the high-throughput detection of antibiotics, which are emerging contaminants in water. The fluorescence responses (F450, F682, and the F450/F682 ratio) of the DE-CDs toward various antibiotics were analyzed using principal component analysis (PCA), enabling the facile differentiation of multiple antibiotics with good reproducibility achieved in real water samples. This innovative DE-CDs approach eliminates the need for multiple sensing probes and offers a practical concept for the creation of single-component sensing platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics, among the most frequently prescribed medications worldwide with a prolonged history of use, have emerged as formidable water contaminants due to their excessive application [1]. The pervasiveness of antibiotics in the environment extends detrimental effects to ecosystems [2], wildlife [3], flora [4], and human health [5,6], particularly through sewage effluents. This has exacerbated the proliferation of antibiotic-resistant bacteria (ARB) and antibiotic resistance genes (ARGs), posing significant threats to public health [7]. In light of the extensive use and continuous release of antibiotics into the environment, there is an urgent need to develop rapid and effective detection methods to monitor these emerging contaminants in water systems.

Traditional detection methods for antibiotic residues include microbial growth inhibition, microbial receptor, enzymatic colourimetric, receptor binding, chromatographic, and immunoassays [8]. However, these methods often struggle with accurate quantification as they rely on qualitative or semi-quantitative analysis [9]. While newer techniques like LC-MS [10] and electrochemical methods [11] offer good detection limits, they require bulky instruments, complex procedures, and skilled operators, making rapid detection challenging. In contrast, fluorescence sensing has become increasingly popular due to its high sensitivity, selectivity, and the convenience of being portable and easily integrable [12–14].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of fluorescent materials that have been widely adopted due to their attractive properties, including ultra-small size, excellent biocompatibility, high solubility in aqueous phases, broad excitation and emission wavelengths, and physicochemical stability [15–17]. However, most current CDs-based sensors rely on the enhancement or quenching of signals from a single wavelength, which can complicate the quantification process due to potential interferences such as instrument settings, local target concentration, probe concentration, photobleaching, and microenvironmental factors [18–20]. In contrast, dual-emission CDs with ratiometric fluorescence sensing capabilities can effectively minimize background noise and provide more precise analytical outcomes [21–24].

Due to the diversity of antibiotics, it is arduous to identify and evaluate a vast number of them using conventional analytical methods. High-throughput detection integrated sensor arrays are more suitable for this task as they have a large capacity and strong anti-interference ability against complex samples. These sensor arrays can provide multiple modes of response based on the distinct interactions between analytes and individual sensors, making it simple to identify numerous analytes [25,26]. However, the complexities of operating and constructing these sensor arrays pose significant challenges that limit their application in large-scale settings [27,28]. Therefore, developing an easy-to-assemble and operate fluorescence sensor array for large-scale antibiotics detection remains a promising solution.

Leveraging the challenges and advantages discussed above, a one-step hydrothermal process was utilized to synthesize dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) from the natural substance chlorophyll. Chlorophyll's intrinsic porphyrin structure endows it with significant fluorescence emission in the red spectrum. This property was harnessed to create DE-CDs by enhancing the typical blue-purple fluorescence emission peak of CDs through a hydrothermal treatment. Instead of utilizing a single material for each sensor channel like conventional arrays, DE-CDs can yield three distinct eigenvalues by incorporating ratio fluorescence. A straightforward sensor array was then constructed for high-throughput antibiotic detection and analysis (Scheme 1 ). This was achieved by monitoring the fluorescence changes of the two independent emission peaks and the ratiometric variations between them. The resulting fluorescent array data was processed using principal component analysis (PCA), which enabled the classification and differentiation of 11 different antibiotics. To validate the practical application of the sensor array, a real river water sample was collected and tested for its capacity to identify and analyze antibiotics. The results showed a high degree of concordance, demonstrating the potential of the sensor array for accurate detection and analysis of antibiotics in complex water samples. The study suggests that the sensor array developed has a promising future in the field of environmental monitoring, particularly for detecting water pollution.

11 Antibiotics including ofloxacin (OFL), norfloxacin (NOR), lomefloxacin (LFX), moxifloxacin (MOX), pefloxacin (PFX), ciprofloxacin (CIP), tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC), chloromycin (CTC), doxycycline (DOX), and chloramphenicol (CAP) were all purchased from Macklin biochemical co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). PBS buffer solution (10 mM) was purchased from titan Scientific co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals listed above were used without any further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ·cm) was generated by hitachi water purification system (Shanghai, China) and used throughout the whole experiments

Starting with 5 g of fresh spinach leaves, the leaves were thoroughly washed in ultra-pure water and patted dry to eliminate residual moisture. The leaves were then finely chopped and ground to release the juice, which was subsequently mixed with 30 mL of ethanol in a beaker. This mixture was stirred continuously for 24 h to facilitate the extraction of chlorophyll.

Post-extraction, the mixture was filtered to separate the solid residue, and the resulting supernatant containing the chlorophyll was transferred to a reactor for a hydrothermal treatment at 150 °C for six hours. After the reaction, the solution was once again filtered to purify it before being placed in a dialysis bag with a 1000 Da molecular weight cutoff for further refinement.

The final step involved the removal of ethanol from the solution via spin distillation, followed by resuspension of the product in distilled water. When exposed to UV light, the synthesized dual-emission carbon dots (DE-CDs) displayed a yellow-green coloration and emitted a green fluorescence, indicating successful synthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent assay of antibiotics by DE-CDs sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: All antibiotics were prepared by dissolving them in PBS buffer (10 mM). 10 μL DE-CDs, 10 μL antibiotics and 80 μL PBS buffer were added to the 384-well black microplate, and mixed thoroughly. DE-CDs have two emission peaks, located at 450 nm and 682 nm. The fluorescence changes (F − F0)/F0 of the two emission peaks were recorded and then the fluorescence changes of F450/F682 were calculated. Each combination between antibiotics and DE-CDs had five parallel replicates. The raw data matrix (3 Tris-HCl buffers × 12 metal ions × 5 replicates) was collected and further analyzed by principal component analysis (PCA) through using SPSS 22.0 software (IBM) and the data plotting was depicted in GraphPad Prism 8.3.0 software.

Fluorescence measurements were carried out through a TECAN microplate reader (infinite M200 pro) and all fluorescence measurements were proceeded with a 384-well black microplate. UV − vis absorption spectroscopy was recorded by a UV − vis spectrophotometer. The fluorescence lifetime was characterized with an Edinburgh fluorescence spectrometer (FLS980). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were collected on a field emission transmission electron microscope (TECNAI G2F20). Also, the average size of DE-CDs was calculated by analyzing the average size of 150 single CDs (Nano Measurer 1.2 software). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained by using a Nicolet optical bench (Nexus 670). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was acquired by Thermo Fisher Scientific (Escalab 250Xi).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure characterization of DE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: By comparing the fluorescence spectra of raw material chlorophyll and the as-prepared DE-CDs (Fig. S1), it can be observed that DE-CDs have dual emission features. The porphyrin structure of chlorophyll has fluorescence characteristics in the red light range of about 650–700 nm, and the emission peak is clearly redshifted following the hydrothermal reaction, suggesting that the structure has undergone some change. Furthermore, a new peak of fluorescence emission can be observed at around 450 nm, originating from the sizable carbon nuclei produced by the polymerization of small molecules throughout the process.

Fig. 1 a and 1b show the TEM morphology of DE-CDs, which are generally spherical and evenly distributed. It can be clearly seen that DE-CDs have lattice fringes with a plane spacing of 0.237 nm, which is close to the (100) lattice spacing of graphene [29], proving that the carbon core has a graphene-like structure. Fig. S2 statistics that the particle size distribution of DE-CDs range in 2.0–5.5 nm, and the average particle size is about 3.2 nm, which conforms to the normal distribution law.

A variety of spectral characterization techniques have been employed to observe the structural composition of DE-CDs. Fig. 1c shows the UV-absorption spectrum of DE-CDs, which has four obvious peaks. The absorption peak of 270 nm is due to the π-π* transition of the CC bond, the absorption peak of 330 nm is the n-π* transition of CO, and the absorption peak of 420 nm can be corresponded to the n-π* transition of the CO and CN bonds [30]. The absorption peak of 670 nm is from the porphyrin structure preserved by chlorophyll itself. These chlorophyll molecules are not fully transformed during the hydrothermal reaction [31]. Fig. 1d exhibits the FTIR spectrum of DE-CDs. The absorption peak at 3346 cm−1 can be due to the stretching vibration of N-H bond, and the two splitting peaks at 2866 cm−1 and 2955 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of sp3-hybridized C-H bond. The peak of 1652 cm−1 is due to the stretching vibration of the CO bond. In addition, 1455 cm−1 is the bending vibration of C–H bond, 1382 cm−1 is the tensile vibration of C–N bond, and 1273 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of C–O bond [32]. The peaks at 1087 cm−1 and 1046 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the C–O–C group [33].

Fig. 1e shows the XPS spectrum of DE-CDs. There are two obvious peaks at 285.3 and 532.5 eV, which are respectively the peaks of C 1s and O 1s, accounting for 79.97 % and 17.99 %, and the peaks of N 1s are located at 400.0 eV, accounting for only 2 %. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1f) shows three peaks of 284.8, 286.5 and 288.7 eV, corresponding to C–C/C–H, C–N/C–O and CO bonds, respectively. Less content of CO bonds indicates that there are more defects and sp3-hybrided carbon in the core, which provides more reaction sites for DE-CDs. The spectrum of N 1s (Fig. 1g) has two peaks at 398.1, 399.9 and 402.4 eV, which belong to CN, C–N and N–O bonds, respectively. The CN bond and C-N bond belonging to porphyrin structure account for more. The spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 1h) has two peaks at 531.8 and 532.7 eV, belonging to the CO and C–O bonds, respectively. The higher C-O bonds content corresponds to the previous XPS spectrum of C1s.

Further research has been done on the fluorescence characteristics of DE-CDs in order to improve the detection ability. As depicted in Fig. 1i and Fig. S3, the emission induced by CDs core structure clearly shows excitation-related emission characteristics. For the peak caused by CDs core, the emission peak stays constant at 330–360 nm excitation, while the strength of the emission rises as the excitation wavelength increases. The emission peak is red shifted and the intensity drops in the excitation wavelength after 360 nm, but the intensity marginally increases when the excitation wavelength exceeds 420 nm. In general, 360 nm excitation is the best excitation for CDs core structure emission, and the maximum emission peak is at 450 nm. The emission peak of porphyrin structure is located at 682 nm, and there is no obvious excitation dependence on the peak position in such excitation wavelength range. The intensity increases with the increase of the excitation wavelength and the peak value appears when the excitation wavelength reaches 420 nm.

Considering that the great intensity difference between the two emission peaks may affect the sensitivity of the fluorescence array, we finally choose 360 nm as the best excitation wavelength of DE-CDs sensor array, and the maximum emission peak of two structure is 450 nm and 682 nm respectively.

Research reports have indicated that changes in pH can affect the local environment of surface electrons on carbon dots, thereby influencing their fluorescent properties [34,35]. It is crucial for determining the ideal pH to achieve the best fluorescence performance. Fig. S4 illustrates the connection between pH and the fluorescence activity of DE-CDs. In acidic settings, the fluorescence intensity of F450 is evidently higher, whereas in alkaline conditions, it is lower. In the CDs core structure, its surface has carboxyl and hydroxyl groups that tend to protonate under acidic conditions and deprotonate under alkaline conditions. Protonation enhances the fluorescence intensity of F450 by reducing non-radiative pathways [35], while deprotonation may weaken it. In both alkaline and acidic conditions, the emission peak of porphyrin structure (F682) is smaller and reaches its maximum strength when pH = 7. This is due to the protonation and deprotonation of porphyrin under different pH conditions, as well as the changes in the environment around the porphyrin structure. In light of the intensities of the two emission peaks, pH = 6 has been chosen as the detection condition for followed research.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The discrimination of antibiotics by using DE-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the dual emission features, DE-CDs can serve as sensing channels for three distinct variables: F450, F682, and F450/F682. Therefore, it is possible to create a label-free sensor array using a single fluorescence source by recording the fluorescence changes of each sensing channel in the experiment. We tested the DE-CDs sensor array's fluorescence response to several antibiotics (final concentration = 100 μM) in order to confirm the suitability for antibiotic detection. The histogram of the fluorescence response to 11 antibiotics is displayed in Fig. 2 a, and it is clear that the antibiotics' individual response patterns vary significantly.

Next, we processed the related data mentioned above using principal component analysis (PCA) in order to extract more data. PCA is a data analysis technique that makes use of the reduction of dimensionality. Many variables can be converted into a new set made up of one or more independent variables by calculating the orthogonal eigenvector of the maximum direction of variance in the data set. Complex data set information can also be concentrated into a lower dimensional space with minimal loss of original data information [36]. In the area of analytical chemistry, it has been extensively utilized [37,38].

The PCA findings are displayed in Fig. 2b. The sensor array is effectively segregated into discrete clusters for 11 antibiotics, allowing for their total separation, as can be noticed intuitively. Furthermore, in order to investigate the potential usefulness of this sensor array, we conducted additional testing with a lower antibiotic dose (final concentration = 10 μM). The DE-CDs sensor array could completely differentiate 11 antibiotics at low concentrations, as seen in Fig. 2c, d, and displayed distinct response patterns to these antibiotics. These findings support the viability of using DE-CDs to detect antibiotics.

After verifying the feasibility of DE-CDs detection, we tested the sensitivity to different concentrations of antibiotics. We selected MOX and CTC as typical analytes, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 3 a and 3d that the fluorescence changes of F450, F682 and F450/F682 all exhibit a gradient change, and the trend of F450 and F682 changes is that the absolute value of the change increases with the increase of antibiotic concentration. At the same time, PCA plots also adhere to a set of guidelines. The data of the same concentration are similar, while the data points of different concentrations are obviously independent of each other (Fig. 3b and e), which indicates that the DE-CDs sensor array is capable of differentiating between concentrations and achieving quantitative detection. Considering that PC1 makes up more than 70 %, quantitative detection of antibiotics can be accomplished by only using PC1. The linear connection between antibiotic concentration and PC1 value was well-fitted (Fig. 3c and f), and the relative coefficient (R2) of the two fitted curves were both larger than 0.99, which proved that the linear relationship was good. The detection linear intervals of MOX and CTC are both 5–100 μM, LODMOX = 3.06 μM, LODCTC = 2.71 μM. Table S1 compares DE-CDs with other CDs for antibiotic detection, demonstrating that DE-CDs have a similar detection capability to other CDs.

Considering the complexity of real samples, we also investigated the ability of DE-CDs sensor array to distinguish mixed antibiotics. The mixture of MOX and CTC were selected and configured a series of antibiotics mixed solutions with final concentration of 20 μM, and the ratio of the concentrations of the two changed from 8:0 to 0:8 in turn, as shown in Fig. 4 . The histogram (Fig. 4a) for combinations of MOX and CTC with varying molar ratios demonstrates that their fluorescence response rises as the concentration ratio changes. The PCA scatter plots (Fig. 4b) shows that they are completely independent of each other and arrange in molar ratio order on the PC1 axis, which proves the ability of the DE-CDs sensor array to distinguish mixed samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism of DE-CDs towards antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: DE-CDs exhibit distinct response patterns to varying detection objects due to the abundance of functional groups and defects surrounding them. A deeper comprehension of the detection capabilities of DE-CDs sensor array can be attained by investigating the pertinent response mechanism.

First, we researched at how antibiotics affected DE-CDs. Antibiotics are a type of big compounds with conjugated double bond structure, meaning they have their own fluorescence activity. The 11 antibiotics used in the experiment were excited by 360 nm excitation, and the fluorescence emission results were shown in Fig. S5. The fluorescence emission of antibiotics at 450 nm is clearly visible except for CAP, while at 682 nm, it is hardly noticeable. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of CTC, MOX, OFL, and LFX at 450 nm was much stronger, which is consistent with previous phenomena shown at Fig. 2. It is evident that one of the causes of the F450 alteration in the DE-CDs array.

Then, we investigate whether the internal filter effect (IFE) makes an effect on the DE-CDs sensor array. IFE is brought on by the emission of a fluorescent material that overlaps with its own or other substances in the solution's absorption spectrum, which has been widely used in fluorescence array detection [39,40]. The UV-absorption spectra of 11 antibiotics have been measured, and the results are shown in Fig. S6. The absorption of antibiotics is concentrated in the range of 300–400 nm, and almost none in the wavelength range after 450 nm, so it can be inferred that DE-CDs sensor array is almost unaffected by IFE when applied to antibiotic detection.

Additionally, the absorption spectra of DE-CDs were analyzed after antibiotic addition to determine if the fluorescence change results from the formation of new fluorescent complexes between antibiotics and DE-CDs. As can be seen from Fig. S7, the absorption peak at 670 nm is basically unchanged, which indicates that the change of optical properties does not involve the porphyrin structure, that is, the fluorescence change of F682 does not involve the static complexation mechanism, and only F450 is affected by the complex structure. The effects of quinolone antibiotics on the absorption spectra are shown in Fig. S7a. It is clear that DE-CDs merely enhance the existing absorption bands at 270 nm and 330 nm without creating new ones. Given the large conjugated structures of quinolone antibiotics, formed by CC and CO bonds, the increased absorption is primarily due to their inherent conjugated structure. It suggests that they may not bind to DE-CDs or only form loose complexes with weak interactions, such as π-π stacking, rather than creating a new fluorescent structure. Tetracycline antibiotics affect the absorption peaks at 270, 330, and 420 nm. Notably, the absorption peak at 420 nm involves CN, whereas these four antibiotics contain C-N bonds instead of CN bonds. This indicates how tetracycline antibiotics might attach to DE-CDs and form a new fluorescent structure with CN, altering the fluorescence properties. The introduction of CAP, similar to quinolone antibiotics, only results in an enhancement of the 270 nm peak (Fig. S7b), suggesting that CAP does not combine with DE-CDs to form a new complex. Moreover, the non-coplanar nature of CO in CAP with the benzene ring explains the consistent absorption peak at 330 nm. It can be concluded that the difference of antibiotic structure will lead to the difference of complex structure and obtain different fluorescence changes.

To further explore the causes of fluorescence changes in DE-CDs sensor array, we measured the relevant fluorescence decay curves. Table S2 shows the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of DE-CDs caused by different studied antibiotics. F450 increases after the addition of MOX and CTC, while F682 increases after the addition of MOX, and decreases after the addition of CTC, which is obvious that the trend of fluorescence lifetime change brought on by the addition of antibiotics is same to the trend of fluorescence intensity change brought on by the two antibiotics indicates that the fluorescence lifetime change is also one of the result causing the fluorescence intensity change.

The material's ability to resist interference is essential for its practical application, as real-world testing environments are filled with multiple sources of interference. The temperature stability of DE-CDs, ranging from 15 °C to 45 °C, is presented in Fig. S8. While F682 remains relatively stable, F450 shows a slight increase with temperature, but this does not impact the overall detection. Additionally, the inherent stability of DE-CDs is demonstrated in Fig. S9. The synthesized DE-CDs were stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C and shielded from light, which is a readily achievable condition in practical scenarios. After 70 days of storage, no significant changes in the fluorescence properties of DE-CDs were observed, as measured every 10 days. This indicates that the surface structure of the material has not undergone substantial changes and does not affect its detection applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection of antibiotics in practical environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: Finally, we verified the real sample detection capability of DE-CDs sensor array. The actual river water samples were from the Cherry River in East China Normal University. After water intake, only solid impurities were removed by filtration without any other pretreatment, and a series of antibiotic solutions were prepared with river water instead of ultra-pure water, and then fluorescence detection was performed. The final results are presented in Fig. S10, where 11 antibiotics could be fully differentiated. Additionally, the concentration discrimination results are shown in Table 1 . The recovery rates of both MOX and CTC are in the range of 90 %-110 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is small, proving that the array has the potential for detection in complex practical samples.

Utilizing natural chlorophyll as a carbon source, we synthesized dual-emission chlorophyll carbon dots (DE-CDs) through a facile one-step hydrothermal process that preserves the intrinsic fluorescence emission of chlorophyll while introducing an additional emission peak. These DE-CDs maintain the porphyrin structure of chlorophyll, emitting intense fluorescence at 682 nm and exhibiting traditional carbon dot emission at 450 nm. Additionally, we have developed a label-free fluorescence sensor array from this single substance, capable of distinguishing 11 different kinds of antibiotics simultaneously. The array's ability to differentiate concentrations and identify mixed samples, coupled with its successful application in real river water samples, underscores its practical utility. Fluorescence response data, when processed using principal component analysis (PCA), enables the clear identification of various antibiotics, demonstrating excellent reproducibility and obviating the need for multiple probes. This approach paves the way for the creation of efficient, single-component sensing platforms aimed at monitoring water contaminants.

Wenbang Yu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yifan Lu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Zihan Wei: Validation. Jing Tang: Supervision. Min Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22274053), Natural Science Foundation of Chongqing (CSTB2023NSCQ-MSX0339), and Medical Engineering Joint Fund of Fudan University (yg2023-30).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111137.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tetracycline antibiotics in the environment: a review
Environ. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Daghrir, P. Drogui, Tetracycline antibiotics in the environment: a review, Environ. Chem. Lett., 11(2013) 209-227.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Concerted Efforts Are Needed to Control and Mitigate Antibiotic Pollution in Coastal Waters of China
Antibiotics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Xie, J. Du, J. Chen, Concerted Efforts Are Needed to Control and Mitigate Antibiotic Pollution in Coastal Waters of China, Antibiotics, 9 (2020) 88.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hepatotoxicity in carp (Cyprinus carpio) exposed to environmental levels of norfloxacin (NOR): Some latest evidences from transcriptomics analysis, biochemical parameters and histopathological changes
Chemosphere

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Q. Zhang, P. Li, X. L. Zhao, S. W. He, S. Y. Xing, Z. H. Cao, H. Q. Zhang, Z. H. Li, Hepatotoxicity in carp (Cyprinus carpio) exposed to environmental levels of norfloxacin (NOR): Some latest evidences from transcriptomics analysis, biochemical parameters and histopathological changes, Chemosphere, 283(2021) 131210.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics: An overview on the environmental occurrence, toxicity, degradation, and removal methods
Bioengineered

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Yang, Y. Gao, J. Ke, P. L. Show, Y. Ge, Y. Liu, R. Guo, J. Chen, Antibiotics: An overview on the environmental occurrence, toxicity, degradation, and removal methods, Bioengineered, 12(2021) 7376-7416.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotics in early life and obesity
Nat. Rev. Endocrinol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. M. Cox, M. J. Blaser, Antibiotics in early life and obesity, Nat. Rev. Endocrinol., 11(2014) 182-190.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Removal of antibiotic growth promoters from poultry diets: implications and strategies to minimise subsequent problems
World's Poult. Sci. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Bedford, Removal of antibiotic growth promoters from poultry diets: implications and strategies to minimise subsequent problems, World's Poult. Sci. J., 56(2019) 347-365.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic-contaminated wastewater irrigated vegetables pose resistance selection risks to the gut microbiome
Environ. Pollut.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. O. Gudda, M. G. Waigi, E. S. Odinga, B. Yang, L. Carter, Y. Gao, Antibiotic-contaminated wastewater irrigated vegetables pose resistance selection risks to the gut microbiome, Environ. Pollut., 264(2020) 114752.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antimicrobial drug residues in milk and meat: causes, concerns, prevalence, regulations, tests, and test performance
J. Food Protect.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. M. Mitchell, M. W. Griffiths, S. A. McEwen, W. B. McNab, A. J. Yee, Antimicrobial drug residues in milk and meat: causes, concerns, prevalence, regulations, tests, and test performance, J. Food Protect., 61(1998) 742-756.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibiotic Residues in Food: Extraction, Analysis, and Human Health Concerns
J. Agr. Food Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Chen, G. G. Ying, W. J. Deng, Antibiotic Residues in Food: Extraction, Analysis, and Human Health Concerns, J. Agr. Food Chem., 67(2019) 7569-7586.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ionic liquid-based dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction followed by magnetic solid-phase extraction for determination of quinolones
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Hong, X. Liu, X. Yang, Y. Wang, L. Zhao, Ionic liquid-based dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction followed by magnetic solid-phase extraction for determination of quinolones, Microchim. Acta, 189(2021) 8.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemically Reduced Graphene Oxide-Based Screen-Printed Electrodes for Total Tetracycline Determination by Adsorptive Transfer Stripping Differential Pulse Voltammetry
Sensors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. S. Lorenzetti, T. Sierra, C. E. Domini, A. G. Lista, A. G. Crevillen and A. Escarpa, Electrochemically Reduced Graphene Oxide-Based Screen-Printed Electrodes for Total Tetracycline Determination by Adsorptive Transfer Stripping Differential Pulse Voltammetry, Sensors, 20(2019) 76.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid supraparticles of carbon dots/porphyrin for multifunctional tongue-mimic sensors
Chinese Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Hu, X. Zhai, Y. Ding, G. Shi, M. Zhang, Hybrid supraparticles of carbon dots/porphyrin for multifunctional tongue-mimic sensors, Chinese Chem. Lett., 33(2022) 2715-2720.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent pattern recognition of metal ions by nanoparticles of bovine serum albumin as a chemical nose/tongue
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. L. Fan, Y. F. Lu, X. Y. Ding, N. H. Wang, F. Xu, G. Shi, M. Zhang, Fluorescent pattern recognition of metal ions by nanoparticles of bovine serum albumin as a chemical nose/tongue, Analyst, 145(2020) 6222-6226.

J. Anal. Test.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. T. Zhang, Z. H. Chen, G. Y. Shi, M. Zhang, Eu3+-doped Bovine Serum Albumin-derived Carbon Dots for Ratiometric Fluorescent Detection of Tetracycline, J. Anal. Test., 6(2022) 365-373.

ACS Nano

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Ci, H. Yang, Y. Bian, Z. Li, J. Liu, J. Wei, N. Yang, J. Chen, L. Li, C. Zhang, An in situ fluorescent copolymer dots-based kit for the specific detection of monoamine oxidase-A in cell/tissue/human prostate cancer, Sens. Actuators B Chem., 397(2023), 134655.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-Yield, Stable, and Ultrasensitive Carbon Dots for pH Sensing and Fe2+ Detection Are Synthesized at Room Temperature
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yu, X. Li, X. Qiao, Z. Zhang, D. Zhang, H. Liu, X.-L. Hao, Z. Wang, H. You, L. Zhou, High-Yield, Stable, and Ultrasensitive Carbon Dots for pH Sensing and Fe2+ Detection Are Synthesized at Room Temperature, J. Phys. Chem. C, 127(2023) 3176-3183.

Polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhang, D. Hou, Z. Wang, N. Cai, C. Au, Nanomaterial-based dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensors for biosensing and cell imaging, Polymers, 13(2021) 2540.

Nanomaterials

Sens. Actuators B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Miao, W. Ji, J. Yu, J. Cheng, Y. Huang, M. Arabi, N. Zhou, B. Li, Z. Zhang, L. Chen, X. Wang, A triple-emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on carbon dots-Au nanoclusters nanocomposite for detection of tetracycline, Sens. Actuators B Chem., 384(2023) 133636.

Molecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Ma, L. Sun, F. Gao, S. Zhang, Y. Zhang, Y. Wang, Y. Zhang, H. Ma, A Review of Dual-Emission Carbon Dots and Their Applications, Molecules, 28(2023) 8134.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Design and applications of carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent probes: A review
Nano Res.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. B. Chen, M.-L. Liu, Y.-T. Gao, S. Chang, R.-C. Qian and D.-W. Li, Design and applications of carbon dots-based ratiometric fluorescent probes: A review, Nano Res., 16(2022) 1064-1083.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-emission carbon dots for fluorescent sensing of permanganate
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zhu, K. Tan, Dual-emission carbon dots for fluorescent sensing of permanganate, ACS Appl. Nano Mater., 6(2023) 21194-21200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric Multimode Detection of pH and Fe3+ by Dual-Emissive Heteroatom-Doped Carbon Dots for Living Cell Applications
ACS Appl. Nano Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Nandi, K. Choudhury, P. Sarkar, N. Barnwal and K. Sahu, Ratiometric Multimode Detection of pH and Fe3+ by Dual-Emissive Heteroatom-Doped Carbon Dots for Living Cell Applications, ACS Appl. Nano Mater., 5(2022) 17315-17324.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Array-based biosensors for bacteria detection: from the perspective of recognition
Small

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Yu, Y. Xianyu, Array‐based biosensors for bacteria detection: from the perspective of recognition, Small, 17(2021) 2006230.

Sensors Actuat. B: Chem.

Talanta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Xie, P. Yang, J. Liu, X. Zou, Y. Tan, X. Wang, J. Tao, P. Zhao, Lanthanide-functionalized metal-organic frameworks based ratiometric fluorescent sensor array for identification and determination of antibiotics, Talanta, 231(2021) 122366.

Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, F. Yuan, X. Li, Y. Li, H. Zhong, L. Fan, S. Yang, 53% efficient red emissive carbon quantum dots for high color rendering and stable warm white‐light‐emitting diodes, Adv. Mater., , 29(2017) 1702910.

ACS Appl. Mater.

Mater. Lett.
